content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

November 18, 1991

John D. Adkisson
Nossaman, Guthner, Knox & Elliott
915 L Street, Suite 1000
Sacramento, CA 95814-3701

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-91-512

Dear Mr. Adkisson:

This is to confirm the advice we gave to you in our meeting
on November 14, 1991. In addition to you and me, the meeting was
attended by Commission Acting General Counsel Scott Hallabrin,
Commission Executive Director Wayne Ordos, Carla Marlow from the
Commission's Technical Assistance and Analysis Division and
Margaret Catzen of your office. The meeting concerned the duties
of your client, the California Water Association, under the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").1

The California Water Association is a non-profit organization
of California private water companies. The Association and its
members have established a program, entitled "Operation Gobble,"
in which water companies will donate turkeys to various local
charities during the upcoming holiday season. The Association has
contacted California legislators who represent the areas serviced
by participating water companies and asked them to identify
particular charitable groups in their districts to which it would
be appropriate to distribute the turkeys. Furthermore, these
legislators may actually attend the events at which the turkeys
are distributed and participate in the events in some manner.
Apparently, in years past some of these events have been well-
attended and have gained significant media attention.

_______________________________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-91-512
Page 2


Essentially, you have asked if this activity will result in
the making of a contribution or a gift to these legislators under
the Act.

The activity you describe is not a payment to the legislator
and, therefore, is not a gift under Section 82028. Rather, as we
indicated to you in our meeting, the activity involves a degree of
planning between the water company and legislator, and could
result either in free publicity to the legislator or in providing
the legislator with a political forum. Therefore, depending on
how the event is arranged, any expenditure made by the water
company in connection with this event could be classified as an
in-kind contribution to the legislator. (See Section 82015 and
Regulation 18215(b).)

Consequently, we set forth for you the conditions under which
we believe these events may be arranged under which no
contribution will be made from the water company to the
participating legislator. These conditions are as follows:

(1) The turkeys will not be delivered to the legislator but
will be delivered by the water companies directly to the families
or the charitable organizations.

(2) In this instance, Legislators may be consulted for
advice regarding which charitable groups are most appropriate in
their districts. The water company may also coordinate scheduling
of the charitable event with the legislator, but will otherwise
organize the event without participation of the legislator. The
event will not be an exclusive political forum for any particular
elected official, but shall be open to the public including any
potential political opponents of the elected official.

(3) Any press releases naming the elected official shall be
sent by the legislator without participation by the water company.
Any press release sent by the water company of the Association
shall not identify a particular elected official by name, but may
indicate that local legislators are expected to attend. At the
event, no posters or signs or labels on turkeys bearing the names
of elected officials shall be made part of the event by the water
company. It is recognized that the water company cannot restrain
elected officials or members of the public from wearing T-shirts,
buttons, or bringing other political material, but the event
itself will not deliberately feature any such publicity.

(4) No representation will be made that the source of the
donation is the elected official. On the other hand, it may be
communicated to the charity that an elected official designated
the charity. The donation itself will clearly be credited to the
water company.

Our File No. A-91-512
Page 3


     (5)  No costs associated with the charitable event shall be
reportable so long as the costs are for incidental matters such as
refreshments for volunteers, press releases, transportation costs
or any costs necessary to deliver the turkeys.  On the other hand,
any significant costs aimed at creating a political forum for an
elected official will be examined on a case-by-case basis.  For
example, if a water company rents a hall or buys television time
to feature a presentation by an elected official in connection
with the event, a political contribution might result.

     Even under the conditions described above, there exists the
possibility that legislators will receive political publicity and
benefits as a result of expenditures by participating water
companies.  However, given the imminent onset of the holiday
season and the close legal call posed by this activity the
Commission does not desire to disrupt such an obviously worthwhile
charitable program at this late date.  Therefore, under the
conditions stated above and for the 1991 holiday season only, the
Commission will not require the reporting of a contribution.
After this year, if the Association desires to avoid having its
members make a reportable contribution, it should restructure the
program to avoid consultation between the participating water
companies and the legislators in regards to the donation of the
turkeys and related public events.  Specifically, it would be
better if the water companies designated their own charities
rather than relying on the legislators to do so.  Further, the
specific events at which the turkeys are given away should be
scheduled without first consulting with the legislators' office.

     If you have any questions, please contact Scott Hallabrin at
(916) 322-5901.

                              Sincerely,


                              Ben Davidian
                              Chairman

SH:BD:aa

sections.question: Essentially, you have asked if this activity will result in the making of a contribution or a gift to these legislators under the Act.
sections.conclusion: The activity you describe is not a Payment to the legislator and, therefore, is not a gift under Section 82028. Rather, as we indicated to you in our meeting, the activity involves a degree of planning between the water company and legislator, and could result either in free publicity to the legislator or in providing the legislator with a political forum. Therefore, depending on how the event is arranged, any expenditure made by the water company in connection with this event could be classified as an in-kind contribution to the legislator.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether the California Water Association's 'Operation Gobble' program, in which water companies donate turkeys to charities with legislator involvement in charity selection and event attendance, constitutes a reportable gift or contribution under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that while the activity could constitute an in-kind contribution depending on arrangement, it provided five specific conditions under which no contribution would be reportable for the 1991 holiday season only. After 1991, the Association must restructure the program to eliminate legislator consultation and involvement to avoid reportable contributions.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is well-structured FPPC advice letter dated November 18, 1991. Question and conclusion sections identified with clear synthetic summaries. Five conditional requirements clearly enumerated. Minor OCR errors noted in letterhead and name spelling (e.g., 'Novetnber' for November, 'Guthner' likely Guthner) but do not affect content extraction.

QUESTION: Essentially, you have asked if this activity will result in the making of a contribution or a gift to these legislators under the Act.

CONCLUSION: The activity you describe is not a Payment to the legislator and, therefore, is not a gift under Section 82028. Rather, as we indicated to you in our meeting, the activity involves a degree of planning between the water company and legislator, and could result either in free publicity to the legislator or in providing the legislator with a political forum. Therefore, depending on how the event is arranged, any expenditure made by the water company in connection with this event could be classified as an in-kind contribution to the legislator.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advised the California Water Association on compliance with the Political Reform Act regarding its 'Operation Gobble' turkey donation program involving legislator participation. The Commission provided conditional approval for 1991 with five specific conditions to avoid reportable contributions, but required restructuring in future years.
